content.text: A Variational Autoencoder differs from a plain autoencoder in that both the encoder and decoder are probabilistic: the encoder defines a distribution over latent codes rather than producing a single point, and the decoder defines a distribution over outputs rather than producing a single reconstruction. This probabilistic framing gives the VAE a generative interpretation — the decoder is a proper generative model p_θ(x|z), and samples from the model require only drawing z from the prior and passing it through the decoder.
A VAE defines a joint distribution over observed data x and latent variable z:

The prior p(z) is fixed, typically N(0, I). The conditional likelihood p_θ(x|z) is the probabilistic decoder — a neural network with parameters θ that takes z and returns the parameters of a distribution over x. Generating a new sample from the model is then straightforward:
Sample x ~ p_θ(x|z), e.g. x ~ N(μ_θ(z), σ²I) or x ~ Bernoulli(σ(μ_θ(z)))

The true posterior p(z|x) = p(x|z)p(z)/p(x) is intractable because p(x) = ∫ p(x|z)p(z)dz requires integrating over all z. The encoder q_φ(z|x) is an approximate posterior — a neural network that takes x and outputs the parameters of a distribution over z, typically a diagonal Gaussian:

The encoder parameters φ are trained jointly with the decoder parameters θ. At test time, encoding x produces the mean and variance of the approximate posterior, from which the latent code is sampled via the reparametrisation trick: z = μ_φ(x) + σ_φ(x) ⊙ ε, ε ~ N(0, I).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.distributions import Normal

class ProbabilisticEncoder(nn.Module):
    """
    Encodes x into the parameters of q_φ(z|x) = N(μ_φ(x), diag(σ²_φ(x))).
    Returns a torch.distributions.Normal object for clean downstream use.
    """
    def __init__(self, x_dim, hidden_dim, z_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.backbone     = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(x_dim, hidden_dim), nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim), nn.SiLU(),
        )
        self.mu_head      = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, z_dim)
        self.log_var_head = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, z_dim)

    def forward(self, x):
        h       = self.backbone(x)
        mu      = self.mu_head(h)
        log_var = self.log_var_head(h).clamp(-10, 2)
        sigma   = torch.exp(0.5 * log_var)         # σ from log σ²
        return Normal(mu, sigma)                   # distribution object

    def rsample(self, x):
        """Reparametrised z sample + distribution parameters."""
        dist = self(x)
        z    = dist.rsample()                       # gradient flows through this
        return z, dist.loc, 2 * torch.log(dist.scale)  # z, mu, log_var

# Usage
enc = ProbabilisticEncoder(784, 512, 20)
x   = torch.randn(32, 784)
q   = enc(x)             # Normal distribution
z   = q.rsample()        # differentiable sample
print(q.loc.shape)       # (32, 20) — means
print(q.scale.shape)     # (32, 20) — standard deviations

Making the encoder probabilistic (outputting a distribution rather than a point) serves two purposes. First, it enables the reparametrisation trick so gradients flow through the latent sample during training. Second, the distribution width σ_φ(x) communicates uncertainty: if the encoder is confident about x's latent code, σ is small; if x is ambiguous, σ is large and the sampled z explores more of the space.

Making the decoder probabilistic enables the reconstruction loss to be interpreted as log-likelihood: maximising E_q[log p_θ(x|z)] is equivalent to maximising the average probability the decoder assigns to the true x. This is more principled than minimising an arbitrary distance metric.
Putting It All Together: One Training Step

import math
import torch.nn.functional as F

class VAE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, x_dim=784, h_dim=512, z_dim=20):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = ProbabilisticEncoder(x_dim, h_dim, z_dim)
        self.decoder = ProbabilisticDecoder(z_dim, h_dim, x_dim, output='bernoulli')

    def forward(self, x):
        # Encode: q_φ(z|x)
        q = self.encoder(x)
        # Sample z via reparametrisation trick
        z = q.rsample()     # z = μ + σ * ε, ε ~ N(0,I)
        # Decode: log p_θ(x|z)
        log_pxz = self.decoder.log_prob(z, x)           # (batch,)
        # KL: closed form KL(q_φ(z|x) || p(z)) for diagonal Gaussian vs N(0,I)
        mu, sigma = q.loc, q.scale
        log_var   = 2 * torch.log(sigma)
        kl        = -0.5 * (1 + log_var - mu.pow(2) - log_var.exp()).sum(dim=1)  # (batch,)
        # ELBO: maximize E_q[log p(x|z)] - KL
        elbo = (log_pxz - kl).mean()
        return -elbo    # minimise negative ELBO

vae   = VAE()
optim = torch.optim.Adam(vae.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
x     = torch.randint(0, 2, (64, 784)).float()

loss = vae(x)
loss.backward()    # stochastic backprop through reparametrised z
optim.step()
print(f"Negative ELBO: {loss.item():.2f}")
returns a tensor with no gradient. The encoder's μ and σ receive no gradient signal. Always use
Treating decoder output as a reconstruction directly:
the decoder outputs distribution parameters (logits or means), not reconstructions. Apply sigmoid/softmax only when computing probabilities for visualisation, not inside the loss.
Not detaching the prior when computing KL:
the prior p(z) = N(0,I) has no parameters; gradients should never flow into it. The closed-form KL formula handles this automatically without needing to construct a distribution object.

Think of the encoder as a witness describing a crime scene: instead of giving one definitive answer, it gives a range of possibilities with associated confidence (a distribution). The decoder is the forensic artist who reconstructs the scene from that description. The VAE trains both to communicate efficiently: the witness learns to give informative descriptions, and the artist learns to produce accurate reconstructions from them.

Before VAEs, generative models of continuous data required either hand-crafted density estimators that scale poorly to high dimensions, or implicit models like GANs that provided no tractable likelihood and were unstable to train. The probabilistic encoder/decoder framing gave practitioners a principled way to combine representation learning with generation: the same model that compresses an image into a compact latent code can also draw novel images by sampling that latent space. This was transformative for drug discovery, image synthesis, anomaly detection, and semi-supervised learning.

The specific pain point the probabilistic framing solves is the gap in a plain autoencoder's latent space. A deterministic encoder maps each input to a single point; points between encoded examples correspond to no training data, so decoding them produces garbage. By encouraging the encoder to output distributions via the KL penalty toward N(0,I), VAEs produce a continuous, densely occupied latent space where interpolation and arithmetic make semantic sense — smoothly morphing between two faces, or combining a smiling attribute from one image with the pose of another.

VAEs also introduced the ELBO as a practical training objective for latent-variable models — a contribution that generalises beyond images. Modern diffusion models borrow the idea of variational bounds, and hierarchical VAEs (NVAE, VDVAE) have produced state-of-the-art image likelihoods. Understanding the probabilistic encoder/decoder is a prerequisite for understanding this entire lineage of deep generative models.

The KL regularisation ensures the latent space is smooth and densely populated. Linear interpolation between two latent codes produces semantically meaningful intermediate outputs — something impossible with a plain autoencoder.
The ELBO can be computed and differentiated in closed form (KL term) plus a Monte Carlo estimate (reconstruction term). No adversarial training, no MCMC, and no normalising flow jacobian is required.
The encoder output sigma_phi(x) quantifies reconstruction uncertainty per input. High sigma means the model is unsure; this is a natural out-of-distribution signal for anomaly detection applications.

The generative model p(x,y) = p(x|y)p(y) can be trained on a mix of labelled and unlabelled data. Unlabelled examples contribute to learning p(x); labelled examples additionally train p(y|x). This is the basis for M1+M2 semi-supervised VAEs (Kingma et al., 2014).
Generating new data requires only sampling z from N(0,I) and passing it through the decoder — no iterative refinement, no discriminator, no score function evaluation.

Using a Gaussian decoder with fixed sigma is equivalent to minimising MSE, which averages over modes and produces blurry outputs for image data. Discrete (Bernoulli) or learned-sigma decoders mitigate this but add complexity.

If the decoder is too powerful it can ignore z entirely and model p(x) without the encoder's contribution. The KL term goes to zero and the model degenerates. This is especially common in text VAEs with autoregressive decoders.

The diagonal Gaussian posterior q_phi(z|x) cannot model correlations between latent dimensions. A true posterior may have complex structure that a diagonal Gaussian cannot capture, resulting in a loose ELBO.

VAEs optimise a likelihood-based objective rather than a score-matching one. Their samples are less sharp than diffusion model outputs on high-resolution images. Diffusion models currently dominate image synthesis benchmarks.
The beta-VAE upweights the KL term to encourage disentanglement but necessarily degrades reconstruction quality. Balancing beta requires task-specific tuning.
When to Use (and When Not To)
Use a VAE's probabilistic encoder/decoder when:
You need a generative model with a tractable lower bound on log-likelihood — for model selection or anomaly detection via ELBO scores.
You need a structured, interpolatable latent space — e.g., drug molecule generation where interpolating between two molecules should yield valid intermediates.
You have partially labelled data and want to leverage unlabelled examples via semi-supervised VAE training.
Your dataset is small to medium (images up to 128x128) and you cannot afford the compute of diffusion models.
Do NOT use a plain VAE when:
You need photorealistic high-resolution image synthesis. Use a diffusion model or Stable Diffusion-style latent diffusion model instead.
You need text generation with coherent long-range structure. Posterior collapse is severe in text VAEs; autoregressive models (GPT-style) are preferred.
Your encoder architecture is so powerful that posterior collapse is unavoidable and you cannot invest in KL annealing or free bits mitigation.

Gomez-Bombarelli et al. (2018) trained a VAE on SMILES strings representing drug-like molecules. The encoder mapped each molecule to a mean and variance in a 196-dimensional latent space; the decoder reconstructed valid SMILES from latent codes. Once trained, Bayesian optimisation was run directly in the latent space to find codes that decoded to molecules predicted to have high drug-likeness (QED) and biological activity. This workflow — encode corpus, optimise in latent space, decode candidates — is enabled entirely by the probabilistic encoder/decoder design and represents the canonical use of VAEs in applied science.

# Simplified molecular VAE sketch: SMILES → latent → SMILES
import torch, torch.nn as nn

class MolecularVAE(nn.Module):
    """
    Encodes SMILES character sequences into a continuous latent space.
    After training, Bayesian optimisation explores z-space to find
    molecules with desired properties (QED, IC50, etc.).
    """
    def __init__(self, vocab=40, seq_len=120, z_dim=196, hidden=512):
        super().__init__()
        # Encoder: 1D convolutions over one-hot SMILES
        self.enc_conv = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv1d(vocab, 9, 9), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv1d(9, 9, 9), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv1d(9, 10, 11), nn.ReLU(),
        )
        flat = 10 * (seq_len - 26)
        self.enc_fc = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(flat, hidden), nn.SELU())
        self.mu = nn.Linear(hidden, z_dim)
        self.lv = nn.Linear(hidden, z_dim)
        # Decoder: 3-layer GRU language model
        self.dec_fc = nn.Linear(z_dim, hidden)
        self.gru = nn.GRU(vocab, hidden, 3, batch_first=True)
        self.out = nn.Linear(hidden, vocab)

    def encode(self, x):          # x: (B, vocab, T)
        h = self.enc_conv(x).flatten(1)
        h = self.enc_fc(h)
        return self.mu(h), self.lv(h)

    def decode(self, z, T=120):
        h0 = self.dec_fc(z).unsqueeze(0).repeat(3, 1, 1)
        # In practice: teacher forcing during training, autoregressive at test
        inp = torch.zeros(z.size(0), T, self.out.out_features, device=z.device)
        out, _ = self.gru(inp, h0)
        return self.out(out)     # logits (B, T, vocab)

    def forward(self, x):
        mu, lv = self.encode(x)
        z = mu + torch.exp(0.5 * lv) * torch.randn_like(mu)  # rsample
        logits = self.decode(z)
        recon = nn.functional.cross_entropy(
            logits.reshape(-1, logits.size(-1)), x.permute(0,2,1).argmax(-1).reshape(-1))
        kl = -0.5 * (1 + lv - mu.pow(2) - lv.exp()).sum(1).mean()
        return recon + kl

# After training:
# 1. Encode training molecules → latent codes z_i
# 2. Fit property predictor f(z) -> QED on (z_i, property_i) pairs
# 3. Run Bayesian GP optimisation in z-space to find z* with high f(z*)
# 4. Decode z* -> SMILES; synthesise and test in the lab
Restrict log sigma squared to [-10, 2] to prevent numerical overflow in exp(log_var) and underflow in the KL term. A single line: log_var = log_var_head(h).clamp(-10, 2).
Start with beta=0 and anneal to beta=1 over training to prevent posterior collapse. Alternatively use 'free bits': only penalise KL > lambda bits per dimension (typical lambda=0.5 nats).
torch.distributions.Normal.rsample() implements the reparametrisation trick; .sample() detaches gradients and blocks learning through the encoder's sigma head.
Match the decoder distribution to the data type.

Bernoulli (sigmoid + BCE) for binary data (MNIST), Gaussian (MSE) for continuous data, Categorical (softmax + CE) for discrete tokens. Mismatched distributions cause numerical issues and slow convergence.
If KL goes to zero the encoder has collapsed. If reconstruction loss dominates, increase beta. Always log both scalars in separate TensorBoard plots, not just the combined ELBO.

Training cost scales linearly with data size. For 64x64 images a convolutional VAE with z_dim=128 trains in tens of minutes on a single GPU (batch_size=128, ~50 epochs). The reconstruction term costs one forward pass through the decoder; the KL term is computed analytically. The main bottleneck is decoder expressiveness: a weak decoder forces the encoder to cram all information into z, while a very strong decoder ignores z entirely (posterior collapse). A well-tuned GELU-activated MLP decoder with 2-4 hidden layers is generally sufficient for images up to 128x128.

Latent dimension z_dim is the key hyperparameter. Too small causes underfitting (high reconstruction loss); too large causes posterior collapse (KL approaches zero). Typical values: z_dim in [20, 512] for images, z_dim in [64, 256] for text. A healthy model has average KL per latent dimension between 0.5 and 3 nats, and at least 80% of dimensions active (KL > 0.1).

import torch

def vae_health_check(mu: torch.Tensor, log_var: torch.Tensor) -> dict:
    """
    Diagnose posterior collapse and latent utilisation during training.
    Call at each logging step.

    Healthy values:
        kl_total        15-50 nats (for z_dim=20)
        active_units    > 0.9 (90%+ of dimensions active)
        mu_std          > 0.5 (encoder outputs vary across inputs)
    """
    # Per-dimension KL (closed form for N(mu, sigma^2) vs N(0,1))
    kl = -0.5 * (1 + log_var - mu.pow(2) - log_var.exp())  # (B, z_dim)
    kl_per_dim = kl.mean(0)  # average over batch

    return {
        "kl_total":     kl.sum(1).mean().item(),
        "active_units": (kl_per_dim > 0.1).float().mean().item(),
        "mu_std":       mu.std(0).mean().item(),  # spread of means over batch
    }

# Example: spot posterior collapse early
# After epoch 5:
# {'kl_total': 0.8, 'active_units': 0.05, 'mu_std': 0.12}
# → collapse detected; reduce learning rate or apply KL annealing

# After epoch 25 (healthy):
# {'kl_total': 18.4, 'active_units': 0.95, 'mu_std': 1.3}
Use kl_divergence(q, p) for analytic KL
SVI abstracts the ELBO and variational inference boilerplate
Good starting point; covers convolutional and FC variants

VAEs trained on sensitive data (medical images, personal documents) encode that data into a latent space that may be partially invertible. An attacker with access to the encoder and a user's latent code could reconstruct the original input with high fidelity, constituting a privacy breach. Best practice: if training data is sensitive, apply differential privacy training (DP-SGD, available in Opacus for PyTorch). Do not expose encoder weights or individual latent codes in public APIs when inputs contain PII.

Reliability concern: the decoder is only well-calibrated within the prior support. Latent codes z with norm far exceeding 3 (well outside the standard normal prior) will produce unreliable or nonsensical outputs. Always verify generated samples come from within the prior region by monitoring the norm of z during inference, and consider rejecting samples with norm > 4.0 as out-of-distribution.
The VAE encoder q_φ(z|x) is a probabilistic approximate posterior that outputs distribution parameters — not a single latent code.
The VAE decoder p_θ(x|z) is the generative model; its log-probability of the input forms the reconstruction term of the ELBO.
The reparametrisation trick (rsample) is what makes the stochastic encoder differentiable — always use it over sample() during training.
Both components are trained jointly via the ELBO: reconstruction pushes both to preserve information; the KL term regularises the encoder toward the prior.
At generation time only the decoder is needed: sample z ~ N(0,I) and decode. The encoder is only used for inference (encoding real inputs to their latent distributions).
